summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's iPhone 16e announcement, the successor to the iPhone SE line with a new naming convention. He examines the $600 starting price as justified within Apple's lineup spacing, highlights the surprising omission of MagSafe alongside the surprising inclusion of better-than-iPhone-16 battery life (likely thanks to Apple's first in-house C1 modem). He argues the 16e is actually a better phone and better value than the $100-more-expensive iPhone 15, thanks to its A18 chip with Apple Intelligence support and superior battery life. The piece also notes that visual intelligence, previously locked to Camera Control, will finally come to iPhone 15 Pro via the Action Button.
key_points: The iPhone 16e replaces the SE line with a new naming convention that ties it to the current generation, making specs and era immediately clear
The lack of MagSafe is surprising, but the tradeoff enables a larger battery that gives the 16e better battery life than the more expensive iPhone 16, likely aided by Apple's power-efficient C1 modem
At $600, the 16e is arguably a better phone than the $700 iPhone 15 — faster chip, Apple Intelligence support, longer battery life — making the 15 a tough sell
Apple's C1 modem lacks mmWave 5G support, but Gruber questions whether anyone actually notices the difference between LTE and standard 5G in daily use
Visual intelligence is finally being decoupled from Camera Control hardware, coming to Control Center and Action Button for older devices
money_quotes: It's best not to waste too much time trying to find any logic to this other than that Apple thinks 'iPhone 16e' looks and sounds cool. I'm just glad it's not the 'iPhone 16 AI'.
I honestly don't know why I'm supposed to care about 5G at all compared to LTE.
'Plenty fast enough, with longer battery life' is a better trade-off than 'way faster than you'll notice, with somewhat shorter battery life'.
Not worrying about camera hardware and only concerning yourself with lighting and framing is a philosophy shared by many seriously talented photographers.
The iPhone 16e is arguably just plain a better phone than the iPhone 15, and I think it's almost inarguably a better value at $100 less.
Just as with CPUs and GPUs, Apple's interest in custom silicon isn't only about price and performance — it's about efficiency too.
We all captured images and videos just fine for 17 iPhone generations without that hardware button. No matter how much you like it, you certainly don't need it.
The lowercase 'e' carries the correct implication that this is a lesser sibling to the regular iPhone 16, not an upgrade or superior of some sort.
key_insight: The iPhone 16e is less interesting as a budget iPhone and more interesting as a showcase for Apple's first custom modem and a signal that Apple Intelligence — not camera hardware — is becoming the baseline differentiator in the iPhone lineup.
